author: @nickpinnetti4107
text:
Any Thoughts on developing graphene based filaments? You Could print curcuit boards? Maybe even print a heated bed? I would be interested in experimenting with it.
On the subject of 3d printing, I have build 4 repraps. 3 prusa style and one kossel. Pretty easy builds once you understand how they work. I have found cura a great program. I also find myself using "matter control" which has cura slicer and their own slicer in one program. It's free and worth a look. 

I also find that the layer height you print at is very important. There is a calculator based on your stepper motors and lead screw that determines the ideal layer height to print at for best quality layers. I believe it has something to do with motor holding torque between full steps. I don't have the link on hand but I found it with a Google search. I believe the prusa founder made it. 

A lot of fdm printing is trial and error. Nozzle Temps between different types of filament (brand, color etc.) I use an aluminum headed bed coated with aquanet super hold hair spray and my prints always stick. Bed leveling is also very important for good print quality. Look into an inductive proximity sensor. I use it for auto bed leveling and have never looked back.

replies.author: @BobEarlRacing
replies.text: Nick Pinnetti Graphene filaments are already offered by Graphene 3D Labs in Canada. I wonder if they going to use this tech in the battery products at Sunvault????

text: I have a good idea(generally) as to how to print liquid graphene into hardened aligned structures. Is this the next step you're going to be taking?

author: @jblistener7491
text:
Consider using Blender (http://www.blender.org/) for design, as it has tools built-in to make 3D printing easier.

Also, when laying the tape, begin from the periphery, and layer the tape inwards from both sides.  This way, you can simply lift off the portions in the center that are actually used, and lay down replacement tape quickly if you're printing multiple objects. 

It's not important for demo objects, but for the kind of work you do, you won't want to pry and/or smack carefully detailed models off the table. :)

replies.author: @stevem153
replies.text:
jb listener Thanks for that JB. I am on the steep learning curve with Blender at the moment it's a great program for design and I'm interested in editing our videos in it too.


Great tip about the tape, makes perfect sense.

author: @MichelPASTOR
text:
Cura is good but you can use slic3r too to slice the model before printing. I find it gives more control over the print parameters. I don't know about the Ultimakers but I've got a reprap clone and it seems like I get better quality and tolerances when printing parts sliced with slic3r. It is important when printing technical parts. I recently discovered that for most printers the extrusion algorithm is naive and does not consider the pressure buildup in the extruder. To circonvent that and still get pretty good quality print I set the speed of the perimeters and external perimeters to the same value. RepetierHost is a free software you can use to slice parts with slic3r and print it just like with cura.

3D printers are very useful tools but FDM printers can be difficult to calibrate and very time consuming to make good parts depending on the complexity of the part. Tell me if you have any question. Maybe I can help.
